stats.headline: **Forces are the #2 most-tested PSLE Science topic across 2016-2025** — 58 questions in our archive, with 6 four-mark OEQs and one 5-mark killer. The trap isn't the concept — it's that students forget to NAME all the forces acting at the same time, or skip the energy chain in non-energy questions.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Forces in PSLE Science
keyConcepts.body: Forces show up in almost every Booklet B paper. The questions look easy in P5 ("which way does the ball move?"), but P6 PSLE raises the bar — you need to NAME every force acting, COMPARE their sizes, and trace the ENERGY chain. Miss any one of those and you cap at half marks.
bullets: **Gravity** — pulls everything down towards the Earth. Always present whenever an object has weight.
**Friction** — opposes motion between two surfaces in contact. **Still acts on a stationary object** — most students miss this.
**Elastic / Spring force** — stored when a spring is compressed or stretched. Released as kinetic energy.
**Magnetic force** — between magnets and magnetic materials. Can attract OR repel; can act without contact.

**The 4 trap patterns this class drills:**
  • Pattern A — Naming ALL forces (incl. friction on stationary objects)
  • Pattern B — Comparing two forces (which is greater?)
  • Pattern C — Spring energy chain + stiffness comparison
  • Pattern D — Magnets attraction, repulsion, and the balance trick
keyConcepts.callout: **Mark-yield insight**: forces questions are short to read but heavy on marking points. A 4-mark answer typically wants 4 separate facts — name the force, state its effect, state the energy conversion, link to the outcome.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Name ALL the forces acting
keyConcepts.body: When a question shows a single object — at rest OR moving — you must list EVERY force on it. The most-missed force: friction on a stationary object. If two surfaces are in contact, friction is there even when nothing is moving.
bullets: **Rule 1** — Anything with weight has GRAVITY pulling it down.
**Rule 2** — Anything resting on or sliding across a surface has FRICTION (parallel to the surface, opposing motion or potential motion).
**Rule 3** — Anything attached to a spring/rubber band has ELASTIC force from the spring.
**Rule 4** — Anything near a magnet (and made of magnetic material) has MAGNETIC force.

**The "stopped on a slope" trap** — PSLE 2021 Q20 (block on slope with magnet, did NOT move) and PSLE 2022-24 Q17 (magnet B stopped halfway down the slope). The object isn't moving — so students write "no friction." WRONG. Friction is what's HOLDING it still against gravity. Without friction it would slide.

scoringExample.scenario: A wooden block rests on a flat table without moving. Name all the forces acting on the block.

fullMarks.label: 2 marks
fullMarks.text: **Gravity** pulls the block down towards the Earth. **Friction** between the table and the block holds it in place (preventing it from being pushed). The **normal** push of the table supports the block against gravity.

keyConcepts.callout: Stationary ≠ no forces. Stationary = forces are **balanced**. Always at LEAST gravity + friction. Add elastic/magnetic if the setup includes a spring or magnet.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Comparing two forces (greater / equal / less)
keyConcepts.body: When an object MOVES or STOPS, the marker wants you to explain WHY in terms of comparing two forces. The verbal template is fixed: "**The [force X] is greater than the [force Y], so the object [outcome]**."
bullets: **If forces are BALANCED (equal)** — object stays still OR moves at constant speed.
**If one force is GREATER than the opposing one** — object accelerates in the direction of the greater force.
**If gravity > friction** on a slope → object slides down.
**If friction > push** → object doesn't move when you push it.
**If elastic spring force > weight** → object is launched up.

**Real PSLE 2018 Q37** (phone slipping from charger): the marker accepts answers using the comparison template — "The **weight** of the phone is **greater than** the **friction** between the plug and the phone, so the phone slips out." Without the comparison, students wrote "gravity pulls phone down" and lost 1 mark.

scoringExample.scenario: A book on a table doesn't slide when pushed gently with a small force. Explain why, comparing the forces.

oneMark.label: 1 mark (incomplete)
oneMark.text: There is friction between the book and the table.

fullMarks.label: 2 marks
fullMarks.text: The **friction** between the book and the table is **greater than** the gentle pushing force. Since friction is the greater force, the book stays still.

keyConcepts.callout: Don't just name the forces — STATE which is GREATER. The phrase "greater than" is a marking-point keyword in this topic.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Spring energy chain (PE → KE → GPE)
keyConcepts.body: Spring problems ALWAYS chain energies. Compressed/stretched spring stores **elastic PE**. Released → object gains **kinetic energy**. Object rises → kinetic converts to **gravitational PE**. The marker hunts for ALL three steps named explicitly.
bullets: **Compressed/stretched spring** = stores ELASTIC potential energy.
**Spring releases** → object speeds up → ELASTIC PE → **KINETIC energy**.
**Object rises** → slows down → KINETIC → **GRAVITATIONAL PE**.
**Object falls** → reverse: GPE → KINETIC.

**Stiffer spring** = more elastic PE for the SAME compression. Compares as: 'Spring Q extends less than spring P for the same mass → spring Q is stiffer → spring Q stores more elastic PE.' (Real PSLE 2017 Q38cd marker phrasing.)
**Fair-test signal** — when the question says 'no wind in the room' or 'same starting position', that's a hint to mention WHY: keeps other variables constant so the comparison is valid.

scoringExample.scenario: A toy rocket has a compressed spring at its base. When released, it shoots upwards into the air. Describe the energy changes.

oneMark.label: 1 mark (incomplete)
oneMark.text: Spring energy turns into kinetic energy.

fullMarks.label: 3 marks
fullMarks.text: The **elastic potential energy** stored in the compressed spring is converted to **kinetic energy** as the rocket shoots up. As the rocket rises, the kinetic energy is converted to **gravitational potential energy**. (Bonus: some KE is also lost as **heat and sound** due to air resistance.)

keyConcepts.callout: Name ALL three energy types: elastic PE → KE → GPE. Skipping any one = lose 1 mark. Real PSLE 2021 Q40 (toy rocket) and PSLE 2022-24 Q13 (launching ball at 45°) both follow this template.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Magnets (attract, repel, balance)
keyConcepts.body: Magnetic force is the only "contactless" force you'll see at PSLE. Two big trap patterns: (a) repulsion balancing weight (like-poles facing each other holding an object up), and (b) identifying which materials are magnetic.
bullets: **Like poles repel** (N-N or S-S). **Unlike poles attract** (N-S).
**Magnetic materials** — iron, steel, nickel, cobalt. NOT aluminium, copper, plastic, wood.
**Balance trick** — two magnets stacked with like poles facing → top magnet floats because magnetic REPULSION (upwards) balances gravity (downwards). Real PSLE 2019 Q38 ran this 5-mark question.

**Magnet on slope** — when a magnet is placed on a slope above another magnet, the bottom magnet's attraction (or repulsion) can pull/push it down or hold it in place. ALL THREE forces act: gravity (down the slope), friction (parallel to slope), and magnetic (toward/away from the other magnet).

**Strength varies with distance** — magnetic force decreases as distance increases. Common MCQ trap: 'Which configuration would lift the most paper clips?' → the answer is whichever has the magnet CLOSEST to the clips.

scoringExample.scenario: Two bar magnets are stacked vertically with their N poles facing each other. The top magnet floats above the bottom one without falling. Explain why.

fullMarks.label: 3 marks
fullMarks.text: The two N poles **repel** each other, pushing the top magnet **upwards**. The **magnetic repulsion force** is equal to the top magnet's **weight** (gravity pulling it down). Since the two forces are **balanced**, the top magnet floats in place.

keyConcepts.callout: For balance questions: state both forces, state they're equal, THEN conclude. "Repel" alone = 1 mark. "Repel + balances weight" = full marks.

keyConcepts.title: The 5-mark killer chain — name + state + chain + compare + outcome
keyConcepts.body: 4-mark and 5-mark OEQs on forces follow a predictable structure. Once you know the 5-step skeleton, you can hit it from any starting question.
bullets: **Step 1 — NAME the forces** acting on the object (gravity, friction, elastic, magnetic).
**Step 2 — STATE the energy** stored or being converted (elastic PE, kinetic, GPE, heat/sound from friction).
**Step 3 — CHAIN the conversions** — write the arrow chain (elastic PE → KE → GPE).
**Step 4 — COMPARE magnitudes** — which force is greater? Which energy contribution dominates?
**Step 5 — LINK to the OUTCOME** — what does the object DO as a result? (Moves up, stops, slides, floats.)

**Worked structure for PSLE 2017 Q38cd (spring stiffness):**
Step 1: Same mass attached → gravity is the same on both.
Step 2: Spring Q extends LESS → more elastic PE stored per cm.
Step 3: When released, elastic PE → KE for both springs.
Step 4: Spring Q has greater elastic PE → object launched by Q has greater KE.
Step 5: Object launched by spring Q rises HIGHER (KE → GPE conversion).
= 4 marks, all 4 marking points hit.
keyConcepts.callout: Skipping any one step typically costs 1 mark. Practice writing the 5-step chain on EVERY 4-mark+ question, even if the prompt only asks for one part.

keyConcepts.title: Top 5 traps to avoid
keyConcepts.body: The most common force mistakes across PSLE 2016-2025 papers. Read these once before practice — each one typically costs 1-2 marks per paper.
bullets: **Trap 1 — Forgetting friction on stationary objects.** Friction is what HOLDS objects still. Whenever two surfaces touch, friction is there — name it.

**Trap 2 — Skipping the energy chain in force questions.** Even if the question asks about forces, you usually need to name elastic PE → KE → GPE somewhere in your answer. Heat/sound from friction is a bonus mark.
**Trap 3 — Not comparing magnitudes.** The phrase 'greater than' is a marking-point keyword. Don't just name forces — state which is bigger.
**Trap 4 — Confusing 'attract' and 'repel' on magnets.** Like poles REPEL, unlike attract. Easy to mix up under pressure — write down N-N = repel, N-S = attract before answering.

**Trap 5 — Missing the 'balanced forces' conclusion.** If an object is stationary or moves at constant speed, forces are BALANCED — say so explicitly. The marker counts this as a separate marking point even when the rest of your answer is right.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to put it together. This quiz pulls real PSLE-style force questions across all 4 patterns plus the 5-mark killer chain.
bullets: **10 MCQ + 5 OEQ** — a mix across friction, gravity, elastic, and magnets.
After you submit, the AI looks at where you went wrong and suggests a follow-up focused quiz on just those patterns.
Mistakes auto-saved — we'll come back to them in 1 week.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

keyWords.definition: The force that pulls everything down towards the Earth. Always acts on objects with weight, regardless of whether they're moving or stationary.
The force that opposes motion (or attempted motion) between two surfaces in contact. ALWAYS present when surfaces touch — even when the object is stationary, friction is the force holding it still.
Energy stored in a compressed or stretched spring/rubber band. Released as kinetic energy when the spring returns to its natural shape.
Energy a moving object has. Increases with speed and mass. Often comes FROM elastic PE (spring release) and converts INTO gravitational PE (rising) or heat (friction).
Energy an object has because of its height above the ground. Increases as the object rises, decreases as it falls. KE ↔ GPE is a constant exchange during projectile motion.

When two or more forces acting on an object cancel out (equal sizes, opposite directions). Result: object stays still OR moves at constant speed. Must be stated EXPLICITLY in OEQ answers — it's a separate marking point.
The force between two like poles (N-N or S-S) that pushes them apart. Can be used to balance gravity in 'floating magnet' setups.

subTopics.label: Pattern A — Naming all forces (incl. stationary friction)
subTopics.description: Tests whether the student names EVERY force acting on an object — gravity always, friction whenever surfaces touch (even at rest), elastic if attached to spring, magnetic if near a magnet. Common at-rest trap: students drop friction when the object isn't moving.

subTopics.label: Pattern B — Comparing forces (greater than / equal / less)
subTopics.description: Tests stating which of two forces is greater and explaining the outcome (object moves / stops / stays still). The phrase 'greater than' is a marking-point keyword. Balanced forces → stationary or constant-speed motion.

subTopics.label: Pattern C — Spring energy chain (PE → KE → GPE)
subTopics.description: Tests the elastic PE → KE → GPE conversion when a compressed spring launches an object. Must name all three energy types. Stiffer spring stores more elastic PE per unit compression. Bonus: heat/sound from friction during motion.

subTopics.label: Pattern D — Magnets (attract, repel, balance)
subTopics.description: Tests like-poles-repel / unlike-attract rule, identifying magnetic materials, and the balance trick where repulsion holds a magnet up against gravity. Strength decreases with distance.

subTopics.label: 5-mark killer chain structure
subTopics.description: The 5-step skeleton for high-mark Forces OEQs: NAME forces → STATE energy → CHAIN conversions → COMPARE magnitudes → LINK to outcome. Skipping any step typically costs 1 mark.
